1: A theme: The prophet awakened from a deep sleep between visions is a type of resurrection — the dead awakened to see divine glory. Christ's resurrection is the awakening of the one who descended into the deepest sleep: 'Awake, O sleeper, and arise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you' (Ephesians 5:14). The visionary state between sleep and waking is the OT analog of the liminal state between death and resurrection that Christ passed through.
2: A direct revelation: The golden lampstand with seven lamps and the bowl fed by the olive trees is the symbol of the divine Spirit-supply that sustains the church's witness. Christ is the light of the world (John 8:12) and the one who holds the seven stars and walks among the seven golden lampstands (Revelation 1:12-16; Zechariah 2:1). The Revelation vision directly applies Zechariah's lampstand to the churches — the congregations that are to be light-bearing communities under Christ's care.
3: A type: The two olive trees that supply oil for the lampstand — Zerubbabel (prince/king) and Joshua (priest) — are joint types of Christ the priest-king. Neither tree alone is sufficient; both together supply the lampstand. Christ holds both offices in unity: 'You are a priest forever, after the order of Melchizedek' (Hebrews 7:17) — Melchizedek who was both king of Salem and priest (Genesis 14:18-20), the type Christ fulfills by being both Davidic king and eternal high priest.
4: A theme: The prophetic question 'What are these?' before the revelation is given models the right approach to Christ: asking, seeking, knocking before the revelation is received. 'Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you' (Matthew 7:7). The interpretive angel who explains the vision is a type of the Spirit who illuminates the meaning of Christ: 'He will guide you into all the truth' (John 16:13).
5: A theme: 'Do you not know what these are?' — the angel's question implies that the prophet should recognize the significance of what he sees. The NT's form: 'Have I been with you so long, and you still do not know me, Philip?' (John 14:9). Christ addresses the disciples' failure to recognize who he is — the one in whom the Father dwells, the one through whom all the divine-Spirit supply flows. The visions' meaning requires divine illumination; so does the recognition of Christ as Lord.
6: A direct revelation: 'Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the LORD of hosts.' This verse is the OT's most concentrated statement of the principle that governs all of Christ's saving work. Christ accomplished the atonement not by military force or human power but by the Spirit: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience' (Hebrews 9:14). The Spirit-empowered weakness of the cross is the definitive expression of Zechariah 4:6.
7: A type: The great mountain that will become a plain before Zerubbabel, and the capstone brought forward with shouts of 'Grace, grace!' are fulfilled in Christ's clearing of every obstacle to salvation. 'I tell you, if you have faith like a grain of mustard seed, you will say to this mountain, Move from here to there, and it will move' (Matthew 17:20). The shouts of 'Grace, grace to it!' at the capstone's placement anticipate the gospel proclamation: 'For by grace you have been saved through faith' (Ephesians 2:8). The capstone is Christ (1 Peter 2:7: the honor is for you who believe).
8: A theme: The double oracle — the vision followed by a confirming word — reflects the double witness principle of two or three witnesses (Deuteronomy 17:6; Zechariah 19:15). Christ's works and words together form the double testimony to his identity: 'Even if I do bear witness about myself, my testimony is true... The Father who sent me has himself borne witness about me' (John 5:31, 37). The confirming word that follows the vision is the Father's confirming word that follows the Son's ministry.
9: A type: 'The hands of Zerubbabel have laid the foundation of this house; his hands shall also complete it.' Zerubbabel both lays the foundation and completes the building — a type of Christ who 'is the founder and perfecter of our faith' (Hebrews 12:2). Christ is both the one who lays the foundation ('no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ', 1 Corinthians 3:11) and the one who brings it to completion ('he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ', Philippians 1:6).
10: A direct revelation: 'Whoever has despised the day of small things shall rejoice.' The day of small things — the modest beginning of the second temple — is the type of Christ's incarnation: 'He had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him' (Isaiah 53:2). The small, apparently insignificant birth in Bethlehem, the carpenter's son from Nazareth ('can anything good come out of Nazareth?', John 1:46) — the day of small things that those who despised it will ultimately rejoice in. The seven eyes of YHWH that range through the whole earth are the omniscient Spirit sent on the church at Pentecost (Revelation 5:6: seven eyes, which are the seven spirits of God sent out into all the earth).
11: A type: The question about the two olive trees — held back until the vision's resolution — models the deep questions about Christ's nature that await their full resolution. 'For now we see in a mirror dimly, but then face to face. Now I know in part; then I shall know fully, even as I have been fully known' (1 Corinthians 13:12). The olive-tree question is answered in v14 with the identity of the anointed ones; the Christ-question is answered in the resurrection and the new creation.
12: A type: The golden pipes through which the fresh oil flows — the channels of divine Spirit-supply from the anointed ones to the lampstand — are a type of Christ as the conduit of the Spirit. 'Whoever believes in me, as the Scripture has said, out of his heart will flow rivers of living water' (John 7:38) — Christ as the source from whom the Spirit flows. The golden pipes carrying oil to the lampstand are the NT's 'rivers of living water' that flow from Christ to the believing community.
13: A theme: The prophet's repeated 'I do not know, my lord' before the revelation models the proper posture before Christ: humble ignorance that seeks and receives. 'Unless you turn and become like children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 18:3). The disciple who says 'I do not know' is the one who can be taught; the wise and understanding who think they already know are precisely those to whom the revelation is hidden (Matthew 11:25).
14: A direct revelation: 'These are the two anointed ones who stand by the Lord of the whole earth.' The two anointed ones (Zerubbabel and Joshua) are the type that the NT's one anointed one fulfills. Christ is the mashiach (anointed one) in whom both the royal and priestly anointings are united: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor' (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1). The two office-bearers who stand beside the Lord in Zechariah's vision become one figure in the NT: the one who stands at the right hand of the Lord (Hebrews 1:3) as the supreme priest-king.
